Name: Disfrutar
Recognition: ★★★ since 2024 · World's 50 Best #1 in 2024 · Michelin Green Star
What_to_order: the tasting menu only — 30+ courses of textural illusions, ferments, and Mediterranean reinvention
Notes: Xatruch + Castro + Casañas, all senior elBulli alumni. The hardest table in Spain. Closed Sun–Mon. The 'false dishes' (one thing tastes of another) are the signature trick.

Name: Lasarte
Recognition: ★★★ (Martín Berasategui's Barcelona satellite under Paolo Casagrande)
What_to_order: the dinner tasting; or the weekday lunch (€225) — better value, same kitchen
Notes: Inside the 5GL Monument Hotel. The 'Il Milione' private counter (8 seats, kitchen-front) is bookable separately. Berasategui himself doesn't cook here — Casagrande does.

Name: Enigma
Recognition: ★★ (promoted from ★ in November 2025 — the news of the 2026 guide)
What_to_order: the 25-course tasting; dishes are not named until after you eat them
Reservation: Wed–Fri eves only, book via enigmaconcept.es
Notes: Albert Adrià — Ferran's brother, the elBarri group founder. Bodega 1900, Tickets, Pakta, Hoja Santa all closed in 2020; Enigma was the sole survivor. Now promoted to ★★ and the elBulli legacy lives most directly here.

Name: Cocina Hermanos Torres
Recognition: ★★★ + Michelin Green Star — the only triple-star in Barcelona with the sustainability award
What_to_order: the theatrical tasting menu — three open cooking stations in the centre of the converted industrial space, you eat surrounded by the kitchen
Notes: Sergio + Javier Torres. Single most photogenic dining room of any starred restaurant in the city. The artichoke with truffle is canonical.

Name: Mont Bar
Recognition: ★★ (promoted from ★ in November 2025 — most discussed promotion of the 2026 guide)
What_to_order: sobrasada and Mahón cheese mochi; the sea-cucumber 'carbonara'; the creative pintxo opening series
Notes: Chef Fran Agudo, ex-Tickets (elBarri). The gastrobar format reaches ★★ — a Barcelona statement.

Name: Aleia
What_to_order: the ingredient-led seasonal tasting — pared-back plates that let produce speak
Notes: Chefs Paulo Airaudo (Italo-Argentine) + Rafa de Bedoya (Jerez). Inside Hotel Casa Fuster, a 1908 Domènech i Montaner Modernista building.

Name: Bar Cañete
Recognition: Michelin recommended — the canonical Raval table
What_to_order: anchoas de Santoña (Cantabrian anchovies), gamba de Palamós (the great prawn — €19–22), fideuà Cañete, the pa amb tomàquet
Reservation: Essential at dinner; counter seats for the kitchen view
Notes: Two blocks from the Liceu. The ringside counter is the best seat. Tapas €5–20 each.

Name: Cinc Sentits
What_to_order: the Tasting (longer) or Essence (shorter) menu — pa amb tomàquet sequence, Penedès wine pairing
Notes: Jordi Artal, Canadian-Catalan, self-taught. The relationships with small Catalan producers (cheese, oil, vegetables) are the spine.

Name: Can Culleretes
Recognition: Founded 1786 — oldest restaurant in Catalonia, second oldest in Spain (Guinness-recognised)
What_to_order: escudella i carn d'olla (the ur-Catalan meat-and-veg stew), botifarra amb mongetes, canelons d'espinacs amb brandada de bacallà, civet de porc senglar, crema catalana
Notes: Agut Manubens family, continuously since 1786. Bib Gourmand 2026. Closed Monday.

Name: 7 Portes (Set Portes)
Recognition: Since 1836 — the paella institution; the Paella Parellada was invented here
What_to_order: Paella Parellada (boneless meat + shellfish, the house classic), arròs amb llagosta, fideuà, suquet de peix
Notes: Daily 13:00–01:00 (continuous service). The 19th-century vaulted dining room with name-plates of historic patrons is the experience.

Name: Quimet & Quimet
Recognition: Founded 1914, fifth-generation Quimet — standing only, tiny, magnificent
What_to_order: montaditos (small open-faced bites on bread — truffled sturgeon roe, smoked salmon with caviar cream, boquerón with anchovy cream); cava or vermut by the glass
Notes: Arrive at opening or wait. Cult tinned-seafood selection on the back wall. Cash and card both.

Name: La Cova Fumada
Recognition: Cash-only, no sign — where la bomba was invented (Civil War era)
What_to_order: la bomba (fried potato sphere holding seasoned meat, paprika dust, allioli + brava sauces), grilled sardines, clams, sepia
Reservation: Walk-in only — no reservations, no phone bookings
Notes: Family-run on a Barceloneta corner since 1944. Cash only. OAD Casual Europe #81 (2025). The bomba in any other bar is a copy of this one.

Name: El Xampanyet
Recognition: Cava and anchovy bar since 1929 — three generations of the Esteve family, on the Picasso Museum street
What_to_order: the house cava (lightly sweet, ceramic jug); anchovies from L'Escala; the montaditos; pa amb tomàquet
Notes: Narrow tile-decorated tavern. Tourist-known but genuinely excellent — the locals come earlier. Closed Sun + Mon.

Name: Els Pescadors
Recognition: The Poblenou seafood benchmark — suquet de peix with authority
What_to_order: suquet de peix (the fisherman's stew, definitive version), fideuà negra (squid-ink noodles), arròs a banda, fresh grilled catch of the day
Best_time: Lunch on the terrace of the old square
Notes: Plaça de Prim is the old fishing-village square of Poblenou. The terrace is the reason.

Name: Suculent
Recognition: Michelin recommended — the elBulli-trained reading of Catalan classics
What_to_order: the 'Los Clásicos' or 'Suculent' sharing menu — reimagined Spanish-Mediterranean classics; octopus, brava, slow-cooked meats
Notes: Chef Toni Romero (elBulli alumni). Casual format, serious cooking.

Name: Mercat de la Boqueria
Recognition: Founded 1840 on the site of the former Convent of Sant Josep — Barcelona's flagship market
What_to_order: the back rows (Pinotxo Bar, Bar Central — locals eat here), jamón ibérico bellota, Catalan goat cheeses, salt-cured anchovies, fresh figs and tomatoes in season
Price_range: EUR variable · the bar counters EUR 12–25
Best_time: Tue–Sat morning 08:00–11:00 (avoid the lunchtime tourist crush)
Notes: Closed Sunday. The front-row stalls near La Rambla are tourist-oriented; the real market is at the back.

Name: Mercat de Sant Antoni
Recognition: 1882 iron market by Antoni Rovira i Trias — reopened in 2018 after eight years of restoration
What_to_order: the Sunday morning book + collectibles market on the perimeter; the produce inside (Tue–Sat); after-market vermut at Bar Calders one block south
Best_time: Sunday morning for the books; Tue–Sat for fresh produce
Notes: The Sant Antoni vermut neighborhood orbits this market.

Name: Codorníu
Neighborhood: Sant Sadurní d'Anoia — 45 min south by R4 train
Recognition: Founded 1551 · birthplace of Cava (1872, Josep Raventós i Fatjó) · cellars by Puig i Cadafalch 1895–1921, Spanish National Monument 1976
What_to_order: the cellar tour through 27 km of underground galleries; tasting of Cava de Paraje Calificado (El Tros Nou, La Pleta, La Fideuera) and the Anna de Codorníu range
Reservation: Book ahead at codorniu.com — slots fill weekends
Notes: Modernista brick architecture, ribbed cellars, a 27-km labyrinth on three levels. The R4 Renfe from Sants takes 45 minutes. Pair with Recaredo or Gramona by appointment.
